result: I keep thinking about the hour just before dawn, when the sky is a bruised violet and even the moon seems unsure if it should stay. The house is a hush of breathing bodies and settling wood, and I am the only one awake, barefoot on cool planks, making tea I won’t taste. Outside, the neighbour’s apple tree is already holding up a single fruit like a lantern, and I imagine it glowing from within, lit by the slow sugars thickening all summer. Somewhere inside my chest is a similar hidden lamp; I feel it trying to switch on, a small click I can’t quite hear over the heartbeat percussion. I used to wait for big revelations—thunderbolts, diplomas, love letters, plane tickets—but now I lean toward these micro-ignitions: a kettle’s first bubble, the soft pop of aberry between molars, the way my daughter says “tomorrow” like “tuh-mallow,” stretching time into taffy. I read once that 90 percent of our cells carry microbial DNA, so statistically I am mostly not myself; still, every morning I wake with the same ache in the same knee, the same craving for salt, the same sentence forming like condensation on the windowpane of thought: Begin again. I never know what it means, only that it always arrives before language loses its overnight shimmer, before the news rolls in like damp fog, before the day decides what portion of me it will keep. I sip the bitter tea anyway, greet the apple with a nod, and practice the art of staying porous until light officially arrives.
